perturbation.original_text: Conﬁdential Information means:- (a) information relating to the Disclosing Party regarding its business, partners, customers or ﬁnancial aﬀairs (including details relating to any software the copyright in respect of which is vested in the Disclosing Party) which is obtained by the Receiving Party, either before or after this undertaking is entered into and either in writing or orally from or pursuant to the discussions with the Personnel of the Disclosing Party;
perturbation.changed_text: Conﬁdential Information means:- (a) information relating to the Disclosing Party regarding its business, partners, customers or ﬁnancial aﬀairs (including details relating to any software the copyright in respect of which is vested in the Disclosing Party) which is obtained by the Receiving Party, only after this undertaking is entered into and only in writing from or pursuant to the discussions with the Personnel of the Disclosing Party;
perturbation.explanation: Restricting 'Confidential Information' to only information received *after* the agreement and *only* in writing contradicts common legal interpretations of confidentiality, which often include oral and pre-existing information. The original phrasing is more standard, but the modification introduces ambiguity as to the scope of protected information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification limits the scope of 'Confidential Information,' potentially excluding trade secrets disclosed orally or before the agreement. This contradicts the broad protection afforded to trade secrets under federal law, which does not specify the format or timing of the disclosure. This also conflicts with the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, which protects trade secrets regardless of when or how they were disclosed, provided that reasonable measures are taken to maintain their secrecy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' to only information received 'after' the agreement and 'only' in writing. This contradicts the broad protection generally afforded to trade secrets and confidential information. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that the modification potentially excludes trade secrets disclosed orally or before the agreement, conflicting with the Economic Espionage Act of 1996. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, indicating its relevance. The change significantly weakens the standard interpretation of confidentiality, creating a direct and impactful contradiction, as the altered clause could fail legal scrutiny by excluding previously or orally disclosed confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: A Receiving Party shall treat and safeguard as private and conﬁdential all of the Conﬁdential Information and will take all reasonable precautions in dealing with any Conﬁdential Information so as to prevent any third party from having access to the Conﬁdential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: A Receiving Party shall treat and safeguard as private and conﬁdential all of the Conﬁdential Information and will take commercially reasonable precautions in dealing with any Conﬁdential Information so as to prevent any third party from having access to the Conﬁdential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'all reasonable precautions' to 'commercially reasonable precautions' weakens the protection of Confidential Information. While seemingly minor, 'commercially reasonable' introduces a cost-benefit analysis, potentially allowing the Receiving Party to forgo security measures if they are deemed too expensive, even if they are, in fact, reasonable under normal circumstances. This is especially pertinent given that the original text does not contain the word 'commercially'.
perturbation.law_explanation: By limiting the standard of care to 'commercially reasonable precautions,' the modified text may fail to meet the requirements for maintaining trade secret status under the UTSA. The UTSA requires efforts that are 'reasonable under the circumstances' to maintain secrecy, which may be a higher standard than 'commercially reasonable' in some cases. This is because efforts that would not be undertaken due to costs may nonetheless be considered reasonable under the circumstances.

perturbation.justification: The modification from 'all reasonable precautions' to 'commercially reasonable precautions' introduces a potential misalignment with the standard of care required to maintain trade secret protection. The Law Explanation highlights that the UTSA requires efforts that are 'reasonable under the circumstances,' which may be a higher standard than 'commercially reasonable' because cost considerations might lead to the omission of precautions that are, in fact, reasonable under normal circumstances. Although the provided snippets do not directly quote the UTSA definition of reasonable measures, the explanation of the law makes a strong case that the change creates a legal contradiction under the definition of Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party hereby acknowledges and agrees that damages would not be an adequate remedy for any breach of this undertaking and a Disclosing Party shall be entitled to the remedies of injunction, speciﬁc performance and other equitable relief for any threatened or actual breach of any such undertaking.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party hereby acknowledges and agrees that damages would not be an adequate remedy for any breach of this undertaking and a Disclosing Party shall be entitled to the remedies of injunction for any threatened or actual breach of any such undertaking.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'specific performance and other equitable relief' limits the remedies available to the Disclosing Party. Specific performance is often crucial in NDA cases as it compels the breaching party to fulfill their obligations, which is often more effective than monetary damages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Remedies for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can agree to limit remedies, removing specific performance without a clear justification or alternative adequate remedy can be challenged under general contract law principles. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 359 states that specific performance should be granted where damages are inadequate to protect the injured party's expectation interest. By removing specific performance, the contract might be viewed as unconscionable or failing to provide adequate recourse, especially in cases involving unique confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for 'injunction, specific performance and other equitable relief', but the changed text removes 'specific performance and other equitable relief', limiting the remedies available to the Disclosing Party. The Law Explanation suggests this can be problematic, especially with confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines specific performance as a contractual remedy where a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations. Removing this option contradicts the principle that the aggrieved party should receive exactly what was agreed upon, creating a legal contradiction as the modified contract limits recourse. This falls under the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because the contract deviates from legally recognized remedies.

perturbation.original_text: These terms and conditions shall be governed by and construed in all respects in accordance with the laws of England.
perturbation.changed_text: These terms and conditions shall be governed by and construed only with the terms outlined in this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the governing law provision entirely, and replacing it with 'the terms outlined in this agreement' creates a conflict of laws issue. Without specifying a jurisdiction, the enforceability of the contract becomes highly uncertain, and if legal dispute does occur, there is no clear precedent or law for the judge or court to follow.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Conflict of Laws - Choice of Law Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 states that parties' choice of law governs unless there is no reasonable basis for their choice, or if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest. By removing the choice of law, the contract creates uncertainty about which jurisdiction's laws apply, potentially leading to disputes over enforceability. Although the contract stipulates that it should only be interpreted with the terms outlined in this agreement, in the instance of a dispute, laws will still need to be taken from an outside source, thus contradicting the original statement in the revised contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the laws of England govern the contract. The changed text removes this provision and states that the agreement is governed only by its own terms. This creates a conflict of laws issue. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'conflict of laws' as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' By removing the governing law, the changed text introduces uncertainty and contradicts established legal principles, specifically relating to the jurisdiction under which the contract is to be interpreted. This removal creates a significant contradiction because it eliminates a key element for determining the enforceability and interpretation of the contract.
